current_fact.fact_text: On April 7, 1989, the Soviet nuclear submarine K-278 Komsomolets sank in the icy Barents Sea after a fire on board, taking 42 sailors with it. Built to dive deeper than almost any other submarine of its time, it now rests on the seafloor—its damaged reactor still a quiet environmental concern in Arctic waters.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

current_fact.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode descend into the cold northern seas; one tells a tragic human story, the other an intimate encounter with Arctic life in the same waters.

queue.fact_text: In 1805, the Lewis and Clark Expedition broke camp near the Mandan people and pushed west again along the Missouri River. Having survived a harsh winter thanks to Indigenous knowledge, they set off into lands that, to them, were blank on maps but were deeply known to the Native nations who lived there.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A river expedition into largely unknown territory resonates with a small crew navigating by boat, sharing resilience, simple joys, and the unpredictability of a journey.

queue.fact_text: Also in 1805, Ludwig van Beethoven premiered his Third Symphony, the "Eroica", in Vienna—a work so bold that many listeners found it bewildering. Stretching symphonic form to heroic, emotional extremes, it quietly opened the door from the Classical world into the stormier realm of musical Romanticism.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: Beethoven’s Eroica was an explosive new beginning in music history, fitting the raw, landmark ‘episode one’ energy that set Sedna FM’s own story in motion.

queue.fact_text: In 1521, Ferdinand Magellan’s expedition reached Cebu in the Philippines, becoming one of the first European fleets to anchor in those waters. Although Magellan himself would soon die nearby, his crew ultimately completed the first circumnavigation of Earth, proving in practice what maps had only hinted at: the planet is a connected, navigable whole.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: An early circumnavigator arriving in a new island world aligns with an episode built as a sea voyage through coastal cultures and songs.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1541, Francis Xavier left Lisbon bound for the Portuguese East Indies, beginning a journey that would make him one of history’s most traveled missionaries. Crossing oceans for years, he blended religion, language learning, and cultural adaptation long before the word "globalization" existed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A long, reflective voyage from Europe to Asia matches an episode about travel as a form of inner shift, with world and Latin influences echoing global routes.

queue.fact_text: In 2011, Israel’s Iron Dome system intercepted a BM-21 Grad rocket fired from Gaza, the first successful shoot-down of a short-range missile in combat. Using radar, fast computation, and guided interceptors, it turned real-time physics into a protective umbrella over cities.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A lightning-fast, high-tech interception has the same charged, high-voltage mood as a rock set built around electrifying guitar and kinetic energy.

queue.fact_text: On April 7, 1954, U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower publicly articulated the "domino theory," arguing that if one country fell to communism, its neighbors might follow. The metaphor, simple but vivid, went on to shape decades of foreign policy and public fear far beyond its original speech.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

queue.match_reason: A single metaphor that steered world politics invites precisely the kind of re-examination and unlearning this episode encourages.

queue.fact_text: In 1943, Ioannis Rallis became the collaborationist Prime Minister of Greece under Axis occupation, overseeing a government many Greeks saw as a puppet of the invaders. His choice to align with occupying forces turned political calculation into a moral fault line that lasted long after the war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A notorious collaborationist leader is contrasted with an episode centered on musical resistance and refusal to comply with oppressive power.

queue.fact_text: In 1978, U.S. President Jimmy Carter canceled further development of the neutron bomb, a weapon designed to maximize lethal radiation while minimizing blast damage. The decision reflected not just physics and strategy, but an uneasy moral reaction to a device built to spare buildings and kill people.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

queue.match_reason: Canceling a weapon of mass lethality hints at a fragile hope in politics; a reflective, optimistic episode about storms passing and not stopping ties into that choice for restraint.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 2009, former Peruvian President Alberto Fujimori was sentenced to 25 years in prison for human rights abuses, including state-directed killings and kidnappings. It was one of the first times a democratically elected leader in his own country was held criminally accountable for such crimes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

queue.match_reason: A Latin American leader facing justice connects with a Latin artist whose work often confronts abuses of power and demands accountability.

queue.fact_text: In 1990, a fire on the passenger ferry Scandinavian Star killed 159 people during an overnight voyage between Norway and Denmark. The blaze, still surrounded by unanswered questions and arson suspicions, turned a routine crossing into one of the deadliest maritime disasters in modern Scandinavian history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: Following a story of chaos at sea, a gently drifting, downtempo episode offers a contemplative countercurrent and a space to hold the weight of tragedy quietly.

queue.fact_text: On April 7, 1945, the massive Japanese battleship Yamato—the heaviest battleship ever built—was sunk by U.S. aircraft during Operation Ten-Go. Packed with fuel for a near-suicidal final mission, it went down in a towering explosion that marked the twilight of the big-gun battleship era.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: The fall of a colossal war machine is paired with an episode where loud guitars become tools of freedom instead of conflict, flipping the meaning of heavy metal and power.

queue.fact_text: In 1906, Mount Vesuvius erupted again, sending lava and ash down its slopes and devastating parts of Naples. Though less famous than the ancient disaster that buried Pompeii, this eruption reminded modern Italians that they still live beside a restless, living volcano.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

queue.match_reason: An Italian volcano erupting over Naples perfectly matches an episode explicitly about Italy’s ‘volcanic’ southern energy and musical fire.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1798, the United States organized the Mississippi Territory out of lands also claimed by Spain, a political compromise in a region where borders were still hazy. The territory would expand twice more before eventually splitting into the states of Mississippi and Alabama.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: The birth of a territory along warm southern rivers connects with an easy, sunlit coastal episode whose jazz flows like long summer waterways across borders.

queue.fact_text: In 2021, the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention announced that the SARS‑CoV‑2 Alpha variant had become the dominant strain of COVID‑19 in the country. A handful of mutations—tiny edits in viral genetic code—had reshaped the course of a global pandemic.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

queue.match_reason: A viral variant rising to dominance highlights how small changes lead to big consequences; the episode dives into how learning rewires the brain through gradual, persistent change.

queue.fact_text: In 2020, Acting U.S. Navy Secretary Thomas Modly resigned after his controversial handling of a COVID‑19 outbreak aboard the aircraft carrier USS Theodore Roosevelt and the firing of its captain, Brett Crozier. The episode exposed how a microscopic virus could strain not just bodies, but chains of command and trust.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A crisis at sea that forced a reckoning with leadership and vulnerability parallels an introspective episode about bodily fragility, transitions, and learning to accept help.

queue.fact_text: Also in 1990, former U.S. National Security Advisor John Poindexter was convicted for his role in the Iran–Contra affair, a secret scheme that funneled arms to Iran and money to Nicaraguan rebels. His convictions were later overturned on appeal, leaving a lingering sense of how opaque power can be—and how hard it is to hold it to account.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A murky political scandal built on secret communications pairs with an episode dissecting our relationship with networks, information, and unseen systems of connection.

queue.fact_text: Back in 1805, the Corps of Discovery’s departure from the Mandan villages also marked one of the first long-term scientific surveys of North American flora, fauna, and geography by the young United States. Alongside diplomacy and maps, they sent home crates of animal skins, plant specimens, and detailed notes that turned wilderness into knowledge.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A scientific expedition that mapped unknown lands matches a musical journey that maps out the evolution of a genre, blending influences into something newly charted.

queue.fact_text: The satellite 2001 Mars Odyssey later made another unexpected contribution: by mapping radiation around Mars, it helped scientists understand how dangerous deep space travel would be for future human explorers. Its data feeds directly into real plans for sending people far beyond Earth's protective magnetosphere.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A mission that prepares humans to drift beyond Earth fits an introductory evening set designed to let listeners drift beyond expectations into open, uncharted sonic space.

queue.fact_text: During the 1906 Vesuvius eruption, observatories around the volcano used seismographs, barometers, and cameras to document its behavior in real time, turning a disaster into a natural laboratory. Their observations helped launch modern volcanology and the idea that even destructive forces can be carefully, patiently studied.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: Studying a dangerous eruption up close embraces the imperfect, unpredictable side of Earth; the episode celebrates imperfection and things that don’t go to plan, but still teach us.

queue.fact_text: When Herbert Ives sent that first long-distance TV image of Herbert Hoover in 1927, the picture was only a few inches tall and built from a tiny number of scan lines. Yet in that flickering miniature face lay the seed of the global, high‑definition screens we now casually carry in our pockets.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: The tiny, early TV image evolving into today’s polished screens parallels the shift from raw, fragile recordings to finely corrected, Auto‑Tuned voices as technology quietly reshapes how we perceive reality.

published.fact_text: In 2001, NASA launched the 2001 Mars Odyssey orbiter, a spacecraft that would go on to map Mars in unprecedented detail and detect vast reserves of subsurface ice. Its instruments helped reveal that Mars is far less dry than it looks, silently rewriting our expectations about where life might hide on the Red Planet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal journey to Mars pairs with an episode about reimagining empty space as creative potential, backed by propulsive, forward-moving tracks.

published.fact_text: During the STS-6 mission in 1983, astronauts Story Musgrave and Don Peterson performed the first-ever spacewalk from a Space Shuttle. Tethered to their orbiting craft high above Earth, they tested the suits and tools that would later make ambitious satellite repairs and space station construction possible.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A spacewalk is essentially a dive into the void; the episode’s theme of immersion and floating mirrors the astronauts’ weightless exploration outside the shuttle.

published.fact_text: In 1927, engineer Herbert Ives transmitted the first long-distance public television broadcast, sending the live image of Commerce Secretary Herbert Hoover from Washington, D.C. to New York City. At a time when most homes didn’t even own radios, seeing a moving face travel over hundreds of kilometers felt almost supernatural.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: The leap from silent wires to moving images mirrors the show’s voyage into new sonic territories, with ‘world in my eyes’ echoing the idea of seeing across distance.

published.fact_text: On April 7, 1989, the Soviet nuclear submarine K-278 Komsomolets sank in the icy Barents Sea after a fire on board, taking 42 sailors with it. Built to dive deeper than almost any other submarine of its time, it now rests on the seafloor—its damaged reactor still a quiet environmental concern in Arctic waters.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode descend into the cold northern seas; one tells a tragic human story, the other an intimate encounter with Arctic life in the same waters.
